categories.display_title: We trust AI too much or too little

exact_candidates.note: Directly captures preference for algorithmic judgment over human judgment.
exact_candidates.title: Algorithm appreciation

exact_candidates.note: Useful complement to automation bias for overdependence on automated systems.
exact_candidates.title: Automation misuse

exact_candidates.note: Fits when AI output is accepted because it sounds authoritative rather than because it is checked.
exact_candidates.title: Argument from authority

exact_candidates.note: Useful for underweighting outside advice, including machine advice.
exact_candidates.title: Advice discounting

exact_candidates.note: Captures confusion when automated behavior diverges from the user's expectation.
exact_candidates.title: Automation surprise

exact_candidates.note: Important contextual page when explanation changes user trust or overconfidence.
exact_candidates.title: Explainable artificial intelligence

exact_candidates.note: Useful general page for reduced checking once a system seems reliable.
exact_candidates.title: Complacency

seed_pages.note: Primary hub for over-trust, misuse, complacency, and monitoring failures.
seed_pages.title: Automation bias

seed_pages.note: Anchor page for distrust, backlash, and reluctance to rely on algorithmic advice.
seed_pages.title: Algorithm aversion

seed_pages.note: Captures deference to labels, expertise signals, and system authority cues.
seed_pages.title: Authority bias

seed_pages.note: Useful for vigilance loss and reduced oversight after trust is established.
seed_pages.title: Out-of-the-loop performance problem

seed_pages.note: Helpful for framing reliance as calibration rather than blind acceptance or rejection.
seed_pages.title: Trust (social science)

categories.display_title: We use AI to confirm what we already believe

exact_candidates.note: Key concept for skewed data entering AI systems and then shaping downstream beliefs.
exact_candidates.title: Selection bias

exact_candidates.note: Captures non-representative data as a mechanism behind biased AI outputs.
exact_candidates.title: Sampling bias

exact_candidates.note: Useful when proxies and measurements distort what the AI appears to know.
exact_candidates.title: Measurement bias

exact_candidates.note: Covers unconscious group associations that can be reinforced by AI outputs.
exact_candidates.title: Implicit stereotype

exact_candidates.note: Relevant for systems and users collapsing diverse out-groups into overgeneralized categories.
exact_candidates.title: Out-group homogeneity

exact_candidates.note: Useful when believable-seeming AI output is accepted despite weak reasoning.
exact_candidates.title: Belief bias

exact_candidates.note: Important for users recognizing bias in AI critics or others before recognizing it in themselves.
exact_candidates.title: Bias blind spot

exact_candidates.note: Fits cases where AI mirroring a user's views makes them seem widely shared.
exact_candidates.title: False consensus effect

exact_candidates.note: Useful for the generalized templates through which users interpret AI outputs and social groups.
exact_candidates.title: Schema (psychology)

exact_candidates.note: Context page for how AI bias is evaluated and contested once users rely on its outputs.
exact_candidates.title: Fairness (machine learning)

categories.failure_point: how our prior beliefs shape what we take from AI

seed_pages.note: Primary hub for confirmatory interpretation and evidence selection.
seed_pages.title: Confirmation bias

seed_pages.note: Best seed for choosing agreeable content and avoiding contradiction.
seed_pages.title: Selective exposure theory

seed_pages.note: Captures goal-directed interpretation of AI output.
seed_pages.title: Motivated reasoning

seed_pages.note: Anchor page for sticking with beliefs after contradiction.
seed_pages.title: Belief perseverance

seed_pages.note: Useful for repetition and social reinforcement loops in AI-mediated feeds.
seed_pages.title: Echo chamber (media)

seed_pages.note: Covers personalization-driven narrowing of exposure.
seed_pages.title: Filter bubble

seed_pages.note: Foundational page for generalized beliefs that AI can amplify and legitimize.
seed_pages.title: Stereotype

seed_pages.note: Useful when historical patterns in data become authoritative to users through AI systems.
seed_pages.title: Algorithmic bias

categories.title: Belief Reinforcement and Selective Uptake

The Magical Number Seven, Plus or Minus Two

exact_candidates.note: Useful when people replace a hard judgment with an easier cue supplied by the interface.
exact_candidates.title: Attribute substitution

exact_candidates.note: Useful for answers that feel more persuasive because they are easier to process.
exact_candidates.title: Processing fluency

exact_candidates.note: Fits when repeated interface exposure makes options feel safer or better.
exact_candidates.title: Mere-exposure effect

exact_candidates.note: Captures how vivid examples can outweigh broader evidence in AI-mediated choices.
exact_candidates.title: Identifiable victim effect

exact_candidates.note: Useful for preference inertia around preexisting or preselected AI options.
exact_candidates.title: Status quo bias

exact_candidates.note: Fits menus where the middle option appears safest because of comparison effects.
exact_candidates.title: Compromise effect

exact_candidates.note: Useful when surrounding options change how one recommendation is judged.
exact_candidates.title: Context effect

exact_candidates.note: Captures how highlighted or repeated interface cues dominate attention.
exact_candidates.title: Attentional bias

exact_candidates.note: Relevant when popularity or consensus cues make AI options feel normatively correct.
exact_candidates.title: Social proof

exact_candidates.note: Useful when later or freshest outputs outweigh stronger earlier evidence.
exact_candidates.title: Recency bias

categories.failure_point: how AI presentation shapes our choices

seed_pages.note: Core page for judgments shifted by an initial value or suggestion.
seed_pages.title: Anchoring effect

seed_pages.note: Captures decision shifts caused by wording and presentation.
seed_pages.title: Framing effect (psychology)

seed_pages.note: Primary page for preselected choices and low-friction acceptance.
seed_pages.title: Default effect

seed_pages.note: Useful for option-set design that nudges users toward one choice.
seed_pages.title: Decoy effect

seed_pages.note: Covers subtle cueing from wording, examples, and interface context.
seed_pages.title: Priming (psychology)

seed_pages.note: Broad but useful hub for AI interface design shaping downstream choices.
seed_pages.title: Choice architecture

seed_pages.note: Useful support seed for intentional steering in system design.
seed_pages.title: Nudge theory

seed_pages.note: Important when surfaced examples disproportionately shape perceived relevance or risk.
seed_pages.title: Availability heuristic

categories.title: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames

categories.display_title: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does

exact_candidates.note: Useful when users extend ordinary mind-reading habits to AI systems.
exact_candidates.title: Theory of mind

exact_candidates.note: Fits projecting subjective interpretations onto a system as if they were objective system properties.
exact_candidates.title: Mind projection fallacy

exact_candidates.note: Relevant when people perceive intention, emotion, or agency in ambiguous machine output.
exact_candidates.title: Pareidolia

exact_candidates.note: Useful for perceiving meaningful patterns and hidden understanding where there is none.
exact_candidates.title: Apophenia

exact_candidates.note: Useful when users fill in gaps about how AI works with oversimplified internal models.
exact_candidates.title: Mental model

exact_candidates.note: Captures unjustified confidence in judgments about what the system knows or can do.
exact_candidates.title: Overconfidence effect

exact_candidates.note: Fits cases where users feel more command over AI outputs than they actually have.
exact_candidates.title: Illusion of control

exact_candidates.note: Useful when people over-trust their own explanations of what the AI is doing.
exact_candidates.title: Introspection illusion

exact_candidates.note: Useful background for turning abstract or mechanical behavior into person-like behavior.
exact_candidates.title: Personification

exact_candidates.note: Useful when lifelike cues make the system feel more agentic than it is.
exact_candidates.title: Animacy

exact_candidates.note: Useful when near-human system behavior triggers strong mind-perception responses.
exact_candidates.title: Uncanny valley

exact_candidates.note: Useful when people infer a purposeful agent behind ambiguous AI behavior.
exact_candidates.title: Agent detection

categories.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is

seed_pages.note: Best direct page for attributing human traits to AI systems.
seed_pages.title: AI anthropomorphism

seed_pages.note: Canonical general concept behind human-like attribution.
seed_pages.title: Anthropomorphism

seed_pages.note: Anchor page for over-attributing understanding to conversational systems.
seed_pages.title: ELIZA effect

seed_pages.note: Captures reading goals, beliefs, and intentions into system behavior.
seed_pages.title: Intentional stance

seed_pages.note: HCI-native page for applying human social heuristics to computers.
seed_pages.title: Computers are social actors

seed_pages.note: Useful for users overstating how well they or the AI understand a topic.
seed_pages.title: Illusion of explanatory depth

seed_pages.note: Useful for the feeling that a digital agent is socially present and responsive.
seed_pages.title: Social presence theory

categories.title: Mind-Perception and Capability Illusions

mission: Curate a Wikipedia-backed taxonomy of human judgment distortions in human-AI interaction, including interaction-relevant human cognitive biases and AI-system biases.
